title: Multifidus and Rotatores
content: 4. Ask the patient to relax the muscle or “let it go.” 5. During this relaxation phase, gently lengthen the muscle by taking up the slack to the point of resistance (passive)—note any changes in length. 6. Repeat several times (usually three). Figure: Anterior and posterior views of the body showing referred pain patterns in the chest, abdomen, and low back. Figure: Posterior view showing the deep multifidus muscles. Figure: Pain referral maps for the mid-thoracic and lumbar multifidus. Figure: Posterior skeletal view of the spine showing the multifidus and rotatores. Multifidus and Rotatores. Multifidus. Latin, multi, many; findere, to split. This muscle is the part of the transversospinales group that lies in the furrow between the spines of the vertebrae and their transverse processes. Multifidus lies deep to semispinalis and erector spinae.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Rectus Abdominis
content:
Origin: Pubic symphysis and pubic crest
Insertion: Xiphoid process and costal cartilages of ribs 5-7
Action: Flexes the lumbar spine and pulls the ribcage down; stabilizes the pelvis during walking
Nerve Supply: Ventral rami of thoracic nerves T5-12
Referred Pain Pattern: Upper fibers: Horizontal mid-back pain, heartburn, and indigestion. Lower fibers: Pain between the pubis and umbilicus, contributing to dysmenorrhea. Lateral fibers: Pseudoappendicitis at McBurney's point
Trigger Point Location: Distributed along the vertical length of the abdominal wall
Clinical Notes: Known for mimicking visceral pain syndromes such as heartburn or gynecological pain

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Iliopsoas
content:
Origin: Psoas major: Bodies and transverse processes of lumbar vertebrae (L1-5). Iliacus: Iliac fossa
Insertion: Lesser trochanter of the femur
Action: Primary flexor of the thigh at the hip joint
Nerve Supply: Psoas major: lumbar plexus (L1-3); Iliacus: femoral nerve (L2-3)
Referred Pain Pattern: Pattern (a): Strong vertical ipsilateral paraspinal pain along the lumbar spine. Pattern (b): Strong zone of pain at the top of the anterior thigh
Trigger Point Location: Deep within the abdomen or the iliac fossa of the pelvis
Clinical Notes: Crucial for movements such as walking up an incline or going up steps. Dysfunction is a frequent cause of lower back and groin pain

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Splenius Capitis and Splenius Cervicis
content: 5. Apply sustained pressure until you feel the trigger point soften, and continue stroking in the same direction toward the attachment of the taut band. This should be experienced by the patient as discomfort and not as pain. 6. Repeat this stroking in the opposite direction. Figure: Posterior view of the head and neck showing Splenius capitis. Figure: Posterior view of the head and neck showing Splenius cervicis and trigger points. Figure: Posterior view showing referred pain on the vertex. Figure: Lateral view of pain radiating to the forehead. Figure: Posterior view of referred pain in the shoulder and neck. Figure: Lateral view of referred pain at the temple. Figure: Posterior view of referred pain at the base of the neck. Splenius Capitis and Splenius Cervicis. Greek, splenion, bandage. Latin, capitis, of the head; cervicis, of the neck. Origin: Capitis: lower part of ligamentum nuchae. Spinous processes of seventh cervical vertebra (C7) and upper three or four thoracic vertebrae (T1–4). Cervicis: spinous processes of third to sixth thoracic vertebrae (T3–6).

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Teres Major
content: Referred Pain Patterns: Deep pain into the posterior glenohumeral joint and an oval zone (5–10 cm) of pain in the posterior deltoid area, which can radiate strongly to the long head of the biceps brachii; diffuse pain also extends into the dorsum of the forearm. Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, muscle energy, positional release, and wet needling are indicated. Advice: Use heat/warmth (especially hot showers), avoid heavy steering wheels, monitor gym activities, and use a pillow at night to hug; plenty of self-stretching is recommended. Self-Help Technique: Review anatomy and identify the trigger point; pause on the trigger point until it softens or the pain eases, then massage the area afterwards. Indications: Frozen shoulder syndrome, pain on reaching above the head, slight pain at rest, pain when driving, and impingement syndromes (sometimes misdiagnosed as thoracic outlet syndrome).

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Biceps Brachii
content: Anatomy: The page includes illustrations of the acromion, glenoid cavity, glenoid labrum, and the long head of the biceps brachii tendon. Referred pain patterns are shown in posterior view diagrams. Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Indicated methods include spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, muscle energy, positional release, and wet needling. Advice: Exercise the antagonists (triceps brachii), reduce the load on the biceps brachii when carrying with a bent arm, and monitor sleeping and work postures.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Biceps Brachii (Continued)
content: Self-Help Technique: Review anatomy and identify the trigger point; pause on the trigger point until it softens and/or the pain eases, then massage the area afterwards. Indications: Anterior shoulder pain with decreased arm extension, biceps tendonitis, reduced extension of arms, reduced Apley scratch test maneuver, frozen shoulder syndrome, aching pain over the front of the shoulder, and weakness when turning the palm face upward. Causes: Repetitive motion injury, sports induced (e.g., basketball, tennis), repeated actions with the arm, lifting heavy objects with palm upward (e.g., weight training), and playing musical instruments like the violin or guitar.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Triceps Brachii (Self-Help)
content: Self-Help Technique: Review anatomy and identify the trigger point; run downward from the shoulder until you hit the trigger point, pause until it softens, and then continue to the end of the muscle (insertion). Indications: Golfer’s/tennis elbow, arthritis of the elbow/shoulder, chronic use of crutches or a walking stick, repetitive mechanical activities of the arms, racquet sports, and weakness in turning the palm face upward. Causes: Repetitive motion injury, throwing or racquet sports, triceps-focused weight training, and playing musical instruments such as the violin, drums, or guitar.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Shoulder Pain Treatment Sequence
content: The page details a step-by-step NAT protocol. Step 1: Study the anatomy and direction of the muscle fibers, including supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, teres major, pectoralis minor, serratus anterior, subscapularis, coracobrachialis, deltoid, pectoralis major, and subclavius. Step 2: Sitting ICT to the supraspinatus and upper trapezius. Step 3: Massage the area generously. Step 4: Side-lying ICT to the deltoid (stroking upward only and pausing on trigger points) and teres minor (letting the hand fall off the bed). Step 5: Supine ICT to the pectoralis minor and infraspinatus (a super trigger point); initially this can be very painful, so ask the patient to let their shoulder gently fall backward onto your fingers.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Posterior Cervical Muscles
content: Description: Comprising the longissimus capitis, semispinalis capitis, and semispinalis cervicis. Latin roots: longissimus (longest), capitis (of the head), semispinalis (half spinal), and cervicis (of the neck). Origin: Longissimus capitis arises from the transverse processes of the upper five thoracic vertebrae (T1–T5) and the articular processes of the lower three cervical vertebrae (C5–C7). Semispinalis cervicis arises from the transverse processes of the upper five or six thoracic vertebrae (T1–T6). Semispinalis capitis arises from the transverse processes of the lower four cervical and upper six or seven thoracic vertebrae (C4–T7). Figure: Illustration of the posterior head and neck showing these deep muscle layers and their skeletal attachments .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Posterior Cervical Muscles (Continued)
content: Action: Longissimus capitis extends the head and bends it to the same side; it also rotates the head to the same side. Semispinalis cervicis extends the thoracic and cervical parts of the vertebral column and assists in rotation. Semispinalis capitis is the most powerful extensor of the head and assists in head rotation. Basic Functional Movement: Longissimus capitis keeps the upper back straight. Semispinalis cervicis and capitis are used for looking up and turning the head to look behind. Referred Pain Patterns: Pain occurs in several areas along the muscle fibers, all radiating superiorly into the head and skull and toward the frontal region. Figures: Diagrams of head and neck referral patterns from lateral and posterior views, showing pain zones on the vertex, forehead, and temple .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Multifidus and Rotatores
content: Multifidus Description: Latin roots are multi (many) and findere (to split). This muscle is part of the transversospinales group, lying in the furrow between the spines of the vertebrae and their transverse processes, deep to the semispinalis and erector spinae muscles. Figures: Anterior and posterior body maps showing referred pain patterns in the chest, abdomen, and low back; a skeletal view of the spine illustrates the arrangement of the multifidus fibers .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Rotatores and Functional Overview
content: Rotatores Description: Latin root is rota (wheel). These small muscles represent the deepest layer of the transversospinales group. Origin: Transverse process of each vertebra. Insertion: Base of the spinous process of the adjoining vertebra above. Nerve: Dorsal rami of spinal nerves. Action: Rotate and assist in the extension of the vertebral column. Basic Functional Movement: Helps maintain good posture and spinal stability during standing, sitting, and all movements. Referred Pain Patterns: Multifidus pain is localized and refers anteriorly to the abdomen; involvement of S1 leads to coccydynia. Rotatores pain is localized to medial pain .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Multifidus/Rotatores (Conclusion) and Splenius (Start)
content: Multifidus/Rotatores Self-Help: 1. Run upward in the opposite direction toward the skull. 2. Work using the thumbs with small scooping movements. 3. Pause on painful knots until the pain remits, then follow the stroke to the end. Indications: Deep/persistent low backache, vertebral alignment problems, facilitated segment (localized paraspinal erythema), and coccydynia. Causes: Poor posture, playing musical instruments, lying on the front with the head propped up, ill-fitting glasses, upper crossed pattern, kyphosis, scoliosis, wear and tear, cold drafts, archery, tight clothing, and depression. Splenius Capitis and Splenius Cervicis Description: Greek splenion (bandage); Latin capitis (of the head) and cervicis (of the neck). Origin (Capitis): Lower part of ligamentum nuchae; spinous processes of C7 and upper thoracic vertebrae T1–4. Origin (Cervicis): Spinous processes of T3–6. Figures: Posterior views of the head and neck showing the anatomy of the Splenius muscles and trigger point sites , .

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Orbicularis Oculi (Continued)
content: Origin: Palpebral part: medial palpebral ligament. Lacrimal part: lacrimal bone. Insertion: Orbital part: circular path around orbit, returning to origin. Palpebral part: lateral palpebral raphe. Lacrimal part: lateral palpebral raphe. Nerve: Facial nerve (VII) (temporal and zygomatic branches). Action: Orbital part: strongly closes eyelids (firmly 'screws up' eye). Palpebral part: gently closes eyelids (and comes into action involuntarily, as in blinking). Lacrimal part: dilates lacrimal sac and brings lacrimal canals onto surface of eye.

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Zygomaticus Major
content: Treatment Protocol: 4. Apply sustained pressure until you feel the trigger point soften (seconds to minutes). 5. Repeat, increasing pressure until the next barrier is met. 6. Change direction of pressure during repetitions for better results. Name Derivation: Greek, zygoma, bar, bolt. Latin, major, larger. Origin: Posterior part of lateral surface of zygomatic bone. Insertion: Skin at corner of mouth. Nerve: Facial nerve (VII) (zygomatic and buccal branches).

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Zygomaticus Major (Overview and Treatment)
content: Connections: Lateral pterygoid, orbicularis oculi, masseter. Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Spray and stretch, Dry needling, Deep stroking massage, Compression, Muscle energy, Positional release, and Wet needling are all indicated. (Inhibition) Compression Technique: 1. Identify the trigger point. 2. Position the patient comfortably so the muscle can undergo full stretch. 3. Apply gentle, gradually increasing pressure until a palpable barrier is hit, which should feel like discomfort but not pain.

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Temporalis
content: CRAC Technique: Procedure involves contracting the agonist, relaxing, contracting the antagonist, and stretching for 15–30 seconds, repeated 3 times. Muscle Description: Latin, temporalis, relating to the side of ahead; a broad fan-shaped muscle covering much of the temporal bone. Origin: Bone of temporal fossa; temporal fascia. Insertion: Coronoid process of mandible; anterior margin of ramus of mandible. Figures show referred pain patterns radiating to the temple, eyebrow, and upper teeth.

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Sternocleidomastoid (SCM)
content: Treatment Note: Repeat protocol 3–5 times. Name Derivation: Greek, sternon, chest; kleis, key; mastoeides, breast shaped. Muscle Description: A long strap muscle with two heads. Fibrous tissue replacement after birth injury can produce torticollis (wry neck). Origin: Sternal head: upper part of anterior surface of manubrium of sternum. Clavicular head: upper surface of medial third of clavicle. Insertion: Sternal head: lateral one-half of superior nuchal line of occipital bone. Clavicular head: outer surface of mastoid process of temporal bone. Figures illustrate the sternal and clavicular heads and their associated referred pain patterns.

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Headache Treatment Sequence (NAT)
content: The page outlines the first four steps of a Niel-Asher Technique (NAT) protocol for headaches. STEP 1: Study the anatomy and direction of muscle fibers. STEP 2: Perform sitting ICT to the upper and middle trapezius (Super Trigger Point), slide down the rhomboids, move up to the splenius capitis, and finally inhibit the scalenes. STEP 3: Massage the area generously. STEP 4: Apply a 'dragging' massage technique to the cervical erector spinae while the patient is supine, dragging muscles toward the practitioner as the patient turns their head .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Neck Pain Treatment and Regional Muscle List
content: Contains STEP 5 of the headache treatment: Finishes with ICT on posterior points of the occipitalis while the patient is supine. The page also features a 'Regional Trigger Points for Abdominal, Chest, and Back Pain' index, listing muscles and their page references, such as Erector spinae (102), External oblique (110), Multifidus (106), Pectoralis major (136), and Quadratus lumborum (118) .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Erector Spinae (Iliocostalis and Longissimus)
content: Referred Pain Patterns: Iliocostalis thoracis refers medially toward the spine and anteriorly toward the abdomen; Iliocostalis lumborum refers to the mid-buttock and sacroiliac area. Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Methods indicated include spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, muscle energy, positional release, and wet needling. Illustrations depict a practitioner applying ICT to the back and various stretches .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Erector Spinae Overview and Self-Help
content: Differential Diagnosis: Conditions to rule out include angina, visceral pain, radiculopathy, and aortic aneurysm. Connections: Linked to the pectoralis major. Advice: Patients should avoid 'sudden overload' when lifting and avoid lifting while fatigued. Heat and hot baths are recommended. Self-Help Technique: Step 1: Observe anatomy; Step 2: Run down from the skull to identify spots; Step 3: Run up in the opposite direction toward the skull .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Erector Spinae Indications and Self-Help (Continued)
content: Continuation of Self-Help Technique: Step 4: Use thumbs with small scooping movements; Step 5: Pause on painful knots until the pain remits, then follow the stroke to the end. Indications: Headache, neck pain/stiffness, decreased cervical flexion, suboccipital pain, restricted neck rotation, whiplash, and a 'burning' sensation in the scalp .

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Myokinetic Chains and Sublinks
content: Discusses how primary, central trigger points in one area can lead to secondary or satellite trigger points distally. It introduces the concept of 'myofascial meridians,' a term derived from acupuncture and Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM). The text explains that the brain and body use neuromuscular strategies to coordinate muscle contraction, ensuring stability and spatial orientation.

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Spiral Oblique Chain (SOC)
content: Details Sharkey's (2008) expansion of meridians into 'functional kinetic chains' such as the lateral, posterior sagittal, and anterior sagittal chains. It specifically describes the Spiral Oblique Chain (SOC), which includes the contralateral obliques, adductors, ITB, tibialis anterior, and fibularis longus/brevis.

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Posterior Oblique Chain (POC) and Anterior Sagittal Chain (ASC)
content: Describes the superficial POC involving prime movers (predominantly phasic muscles with Type I fibers). It also details the Anterior Sagittal Chain (ASC), which encompasses the dorsal foot surface, tibial periosteum, rectus femoris, anterior inferior iliac spine (AIIS), pubic tubercle, rectus abdominis, SCM, and mastoid process. Includes Figures 6.10 and 6.11.

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Deep Anterior Chain (DAC)
content: Lists the structures within the DAC, such as the inner arch of the plantar surface, tibialis posterior, adductors, psoas major, diaphragm, mediastinum, scalenes, and mandible. This chain relates to the deep internal structures and fascia. Includes Figure 6.12.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: The Niel-Asher Technique® (NAT)
content: Introduces NAT as an advanced trigger point technique utilizing neurophysiology. It treats trigger points not just as muscular knots but as 'inputs' to the central nervous system. The technique combines deep stroking massage and compression/inhibition techniques in a choreographed algorithm to generate a novel 'neural signature' in the spinal cord and somatic cortices.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: 3-D Release and Compensatory Mechanisms
content: Explains the importance of a '3-D release,' which involves treating the antagonist as well as the agonist. Because muscles operate in functional units (agonist, antagonist, fixator, synergist), a trigger point in one group forces the others to compensate. Key factors mentioned include reciprocal inhibition, pure facilitation, and co-facilitation.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: 3-D NAT Sequences and Hip Treatment
content: Provides a practical example of a basic NAT treatment protocol for mild to moderate osteoarthritic hip (O/A hip). Steps include assessing Range of Motion (ROM), examining for trigger points, and treating the adductors with deep stroking massage from the knee to the groin. Includes Figure 6.13 showing supine inhibition of the pectineus.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Altered Antagonism
content: Discusses clinical observations regarding altered antagonistic pairings in patients with acute shoulder pain. Specifically, the typical biceps/triceps pairing may fail, with the biceps brachii pairing with the infraspinatus instead. NAT protocols are designed to account for these functional antagonistic shifts during treatment.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Regional Trigger Points for Head and Neck Pain Index
content: Provides a regional index of muscles associated with head and neck pain, including Digastric (90), Erector spinae (102), Masseter (82), Scalenes (92), SCM (94), and Temporalis (84). Includes a detailed anatomical illustration of the neck and jaw muscles.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Occipitofrontalis Overview
content: Relates a 'True Story' about osteopathy founder Andrew Taylor-Still curing his headaches. Advice for patients includes avoiding frowning and wrinkling the forehead. It begins the procedure for the Post-Isometric Relaxation (PIR) technique, used for subacute to chronic settings, starting with identifying the trigger point and positioning the patient.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: The Niel-Asher Technique® (NAT)
content: Introduces NAT as an advanced trigger point technique utilizing neurophysiology . It treats trigger points not just as muscular knots but as 'inputs' to the spinal cord and central nervous system (CNS) . The technique utilizes deep stroking massage and compression/inhibition techniques in a choreographed algorithm to generate novel neural feedback .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: NAT Theory
content: NAT intentionally stimulates mechanoreceptors around trigger points and joints to generate a novel 'neural signature' affecting the spinal cord and somatic cortices . It deliberately utilizes automatic reflexes such as reciprocal inhibition, post-isometric relaxation, autonomic responses, and neuroplasticity to release holding patterns and normalize motor unit output . It is an evidence-based approach used for a wide range of musculoskeletal conditions .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: 3-D Release
content: Explains the '3-D release' concept, where it is essential to treat trigger points in the antagonist muscle as well as the agonist . Muscles operate in functional units (agonist, antagonist, fixator), and trigger points in one group force others to compensate . Key factors in this process include reciprocal inhibition (switching off the antagonist), pure facilitation (strengthening the antagonist), and co-facilitation (routing power to secondary teams) .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: 3-D NAT Sequences
content: Provides a practical example of a basic NAT treatment for a mild to moderate osteoarthritic hip (O/A hip) . The protocol includes: 1. Assess Range of Motion (ROM); 2. Examine muscles for trigger points; 3. Treat adductor trigger points with the patient in a side-lying position using deep stroking massage in one direction from knee to groin . Includes Figure 6.13 depicting supine inhibition of the pectineus [4, 5].

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Altered Antagonism
content: Discusses clinical observations that in acute shoulder pain, standard muscle pairings like the biceps brachii and triceps brachii stop operating normally . Instead, new functional pairings emerge: biceps brachii pairs with the infraspinatus, and triceps brachii pairs with the pectoralis minor . NAT protocols are designed to account for these functional antagonistic shifts .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Altered Antagonism (Continued)
content: Explains that stimulating trigger points in a functional antagonist can reflect pain and reproduce symptoms in the agonist . For example, in frozen shoulder patients, stimulating the infraspinatus can refer pain to the deltoid and the long head of the biceps brachii . Includes Figure 6.14 showing functional antagonist reflex patterns for the 'capsular pattern' of frozen shoulder .

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Regional Trigger Points for Head and Neck Pain
content: Provides an index of muscles associated with head and neck pain with page references: Digastric (90), Erector spinae (102), Frontalis (76), Levator scapulae (128), Masseter (82), Obliquus capitis inferior (96), Occipitalis (76), Orbicularis oculi (78), (Lateral) pterygoid (86), (Medial) pterygoid (88), Semispinalis capitis (104), Semispinalis cervicis (104), Splenius capitis (108), Splenius cervicis (108), Sternocleidomastoid (94), Temporalis (84), Trapezius (126), and Zygomaticus major (80) .

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Common Referred Pain Patterns for Head and Neck Pain
content: Lists symptoms and associated muscles: Toothache (Masseter, Digastric, Temporalis); Eye region pain (Orbicularis oculi, Masseter, Suboccipitals, Trapezius, etc.); Cheek and jaw pain (SCM, Masseter, Pterygoids, etc.); TMJ and ear pain (Masseter, Pterygoids, SCM, Zygomaticus major); Front of neck pain (SCM, Digastric, Medial pterygoid); and Back of neck pain (Trapezius, Levator scapulae, Multifidus, Splenius cervicis, Infraspinatus) .

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Occipitofrontalis Overview
content: Details for Occipitofrontalis. Indications: Headache, earache, eye/eyebrow/eyelid pain, 'jumping text' when reading, tension headache, and wrinkly forehead . Causes: Anxiety, overwork, computer use, and wrong glasses . Connections: Suboccipital muscles, SCM (clavicular division), semispinalis capitis, zygomaticus major, and scalenes . Differential Diagnosis: Scalp tingling or Greater occipital nerve entrapment .

chapter: Muscles of the Face, Head, and Neck
title: Occipitofrontalis Procedure and Story
content: Relates 'A True Story' about Andrew Taylor-Still curing his headaches by sleeping with a rope against his neck . Advice: Avoid frowning and wrinkling the forehead . Begins the Post-Isometric (PIR) Technique for subacute to chronic settings, starting with identifying the trigger point and positioning the patient .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Putting It All Together
content: This section opens with a quote from Dr. Andrew Taylor Still, the founder of osteopathic medicine. The text explains that trigger points rarely develop in isolation and often return if the underlying cause is not addressed . Long-standing trigger points can lead to secondary changes in the nervous system, known as sensitization, and cause trigger point formation in areas remote from the original problem . Identifying the 'why' behind these mechanical systems is essential for effective therapy [1, 2].

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Protective Mechanisms
content: The body has built-in protective mechanisms prewired into the nervous system, such as withdrawing a hand from heat or moving away from unpleasant smells . Mechanical pain is relayed to the brain via mechanoreceptors, which respond by initiating efficient movements . Muscle groups are hierarchically arranged into functional units consisting of agonists, antagonists, fixators, and synergists to manage these responses .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Switch-off Mechanisms and Sensitization
content: In myofascial dysfunction, 'switch-off' mechanisms act to avoid noxious stimuli by recruiting synergists and agonists in less efficient ways . While this is effective in the short term, it can lead to neuroplastic changes in the brain and spinal cord over time, a process referred to as sensitization . These reflexes are maintained both locally and centrally within the nervous system .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Pain as a Motivator
content: Pain is described as a highly motivating 'alarm bell' for the nervous system . When the body experiences acute or chronic pain in a joint or the myofascial matrix, it 'switches off' around the stimulus, a phenomenon visible in conditions like frozen shoulder, slipped discs, or around fracture sites . This forces the brain to adapt and compensate once normal feedback mechanisms are altered .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Trigger Points on Demand (TODs)
content: Research suggests that polymodal receptors (PMRs) may be 'sensitized neural structures' or trigger points already in situ . The brain can switch on these 'trigger points on demand' (TODs) as a protective mechanism to rapidly decrease muscle power around an injury, such as a fracture . This serves as part of the body's essential defense, protect, and repair feedback loop .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Holding Patterns
content: The text uses the analogy of an airplane in a 'holding pattern' to describe how the myofascial framework adapts and changes in response to symptoms . Over time, as normal muscle function fails, multiple trigger points form and the protective patterns become more rigid . Both central and peripheral sensitization play roles in maintaining these established infrastructure changes .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Contextualizing Trigger Points
content: Practitioners are encouraged to act like detectives, finding the tissues causing symptoms and observing how the body has adapted to compensate . This requires a holistic view of the patient, taking into account the body, organs, bones, posture, nutrition, psychological state, and general wellbeing to understand why the body’s tolerance or compensation has broken down .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Complexity Theory
content: Complexity theory and chaos theory offer a framework for understanding complex systems where the connectivity is 'more than the sum of the parts' . This pan-disciplinary approach is deterministic and grounded in measurable mathematics . It provides a conceptual model for understanding the 'why' of trigger point formation within the interconnected musculoskeletal system .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Patterns in Complex Systems
content: Stephen Wolfram’s 1984 experiments with 'cellular automata' demonstrated that tampering with variables in a simulated population of cells causes certain patterns to emerge repeatedly . These patterns exhibited lifelike behavior, suggesting that complex systems naturally produce recurring organizational structures . Figures 6.3, 6.4, and 6.5 illustrate these emerging attractor patterns .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Strange Attractors and PMRs
content: The musculoskeletal system, as a complex system, exhibits point, periodic, and strange attractors . These attractors are hypothesized to be 'polymodal receptors' (PMRs)—nociceptors that exist as free nerve endings throughout various tissues . These PMRs are 'switched on' by physiological stimuli, becoming tender and morphing into the structures recognized as trigger points .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Rectus Abdominis (Continued)
content: Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Spray and stretch, Dry needling, Deep stroking massage, Compression, Muscle energy, Positional release, and Wet needling are indicated. Advice: Focus on maintaining a healthy weight. Self-Help Technique: 1. Observe muscle fiber directions; 2. Run downward from the rib cage, identifying painful spots; 3. Use thumbs with small scooping movements; 4. Pause on painful knots until the pain remits. Indications: Heartburn, colic, dysmenorrhea, nausea, vomiting, sense of fullness, horizontal back pain, lower crossed pattern, rib pain, testicular pain, and diaphragm/breathing issues.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Iliopsoas
content: Description: Comprises the psoas major and iliacus muscles. Psoas minor is noted as being absent in about 40% of people and having little function. Bilateral contraction of psoas major increases lumbar lordosis. Origin (Psoas major): Transverse processes of all lumbar vertebrae (L1–5); bodies of T12 and all lumbar vertebrae, and the associated intervertebral discs. Figure: Detailed anatomical drawings of the Psoas major and Iliacus muscles in relation to the spine and pelvis.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Iliopsoas (Overview and Treatment)
content: Basic Functional Movement: Climbing steps or walking up an incline. Referred Pain Patterns: (a) Strong vertical ipsilateral paraspinal pain along the lumbar spine; (b) Strong zone of pain at the top of the anterior thigh. Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Spray and stretch, Dry needling, Deep stroking massage, Compression, Muscle energy, and Positional release are indicated. Figures illustrate supine ICT and various stretching procedures.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Iliopsoas (Indications and Causes)
content: Self-Help (Continued): Step 5 involves pausing on painful knots until the pain remits, following the stroke to the end, then stretching. Indications: Low back pain, groin pain, increased lumbar lordosis, anterior thigh pain, pain when moving from lying to sitting, scoliosis, and pelvic asymmetry. Causes: Pregnancy, emotional overload, disc problems, repetitive strain (running, gardening), occupational positions, soft mattresses, trauma, weak abdominals, and leg length discrepancy (PSLE).

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Diaphragm Overview
content: Treatment Note: Steps 3-5 involve maintaining steady pressure on trigger points until the knee is flat. Indications: 'Stitch' pain when running, heart/lung issues, anxiety, hyperventilation syndrome, asthma, and COPD. Causes: Emotional overload, lower back disc problems, trauma, weak abdominals, smoking, and slumped posture. Connections: Serratus anterior, intercostals, rectus abdominis, arcuate ligaments, and obliques. Figures: Anatomical drawings of the diaphragm and its referred pain patterns in the chest and abdomen.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Diaphragm Details
content: Name Derivation: Greek dia (across) and phragma (partition/wall). Description: A thin musculotendinous structure separating the thoracic and abdominal cavities. Origin: Sternal portion (back of xiphoid process), Costal portion (inner surfaces of lower six ribs/cartilages), and Lumbar portion (L1–3 and arcuate ligaments). Insertion: All fibers converge and attach onto a central tendon.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Aberrant Breathing and Trigger Point Formation
content: Garland (1994) suggested a sequence of musculoskeletal changes resulting from chronic upper chest respiration, including: restricted thoracic spine mobility, trigger point formation in the scalenes, upper trapezius, and levator scapulae; a stiff cervical spine; changes in the tone of the diaphragm and transversus abdominis; and imbalance between weakened abdominals and hypertonic erector spinae, potentially leading to pelvic floor weakness.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Low Back Pain Indications and Anatomy
content: Treatment Note: Steps 5-6 involve pausing as a barrier is met and repeating on the opposite side. Indications: Low back pain is noted as ubiquitous, affecting 70% of people and causing significant economic loss. It can be acute or chronic (over four months). Figures: Anatomical drawings of the posterior trunk muscles (Spinalis, Latissimus dorsi, Iliocostalis, etc.) and photos showing ICT being applied to the low back and abdominal regions.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Low Back Pain Treatment Sequence (NAT)
content: Explains that trigger point release is highly effective for low back pain when combined with vertebral adjustments, analysis of gait, and posture. STEP 1: Study the anatomy and direction of the muscle fibers. The text emphasizes a holistic approach to managing regional pain intensity.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Low Back Pain (Continued) and Shoulder Index
content: Details Steps 2-7 of the NAT low back protocol: STEP 2: Prone ICT to Gluteus medius (STP); STEP 3: Massage low back; STEP 4: Prone ICT to Multifidus and lumbar erector spinae; STEP 5: Massage spinal muscles; STEP 6: Supine ICT to Rectus abdominis and diaphragm; STEP 7: Repeat 3 times. The page also features an index for 'Regional Trigger Points for Shoulder and Upper Arm Pain' and a drawing of the Rhomboids.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Supraspinatus (Continued)
content: Basic Functional Movement: Holding a shopping bag away from the side of the body . Referred Pain Patterns: (a) Belly: Deep ache in the 'regimental badge' area (4–6 cm). This ellipse leads to a zone of pain in the lateral epicondyle/radial head, with diffuse pain into the lateral forearm. (b) Insertion: Localized zone of pain (5–8 cm) over the deltoid . The page also includes illustrations for practitioner hands-on techniques, including dry needling and (Inhibition) Compression Technique .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Infraspinatus Treatment and Overview
content: Differential Diagnosis: Biceps tendonitis, C5–C6 neuropathy, and suprascapular nerve dysfunction . Connections: Associated with the infraspinatus, subscapularis, levator scapulae, pectorals, long head of biceps brachii, anterior deltoid, teres major, and latissimus dorsi. It is often involved in rotator cuff issues and biceps tendonitis . Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Strongly indicated methods include dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, muscle energy, positional release, and wet needling; spray and stretch is indicated . (Inhibition) Compression Technique Procedure: 1. Identify the trigger point. 2. Place the patient in a comfortable position where the affected muscle can undergo full stretch .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Teres Minor
content: Basic Functional Movement: Brushing back hair . Referred Pain Patterns: Localized zone (2–5 cm) of intense pain in the regimental badge area, with a more diffuse elliptical zone of pain spreading in the posterolateral upper extremity above the elbow . Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Methods indicated include spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, muscle energy, and positional release; wet needling is also mentioned . Advice: Maintain good posture (avoid round shoulders), monitor arm position during sleep, and avoid overload. Self-stretching is recommended .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Teres Minor (Continued)
content: Differential Diagnosis: C8–T1 radiculopathy, rotator cuff tendinopathy, shoulder–wrist–hand syndrome, subacromial/deltoid bursitis, shoulder impingement syndromes (painful arc), and acromioclavicular joint dysfunction . Connections: Linked to the infraspinatus . (Inhibition) Compression Technique Procedure: 1. Identify the trigger point. 2. Position the patient comfortably for full muscle stretch. 3. Apply gentle, gradually increasing pressure until a palpable barrier is hit, experienced as discomfort rather than pain .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Subscapularis Overview
content: Differential Diagnosis: Impingement syndromes, rotator cuff dysfunctions, thoracic outlet syndromes, cervical radiculopathy (C7), and cardiopulmonary pathology . Connections: Infraspinatus, pectorals, teres minor, latissimus dorsi, triceps brachii, posterior deltoid, and supraspinatus . Reciprocal Inhibition (RI) Technique (Indications: acute settings): 1. Identify the host muscle and take it into relaxation. 2. Ask the patient to contract the antagonist muscle against 35–45% isometric resistance .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Teres Major
content: Name Derivation: Latin 'teres' (rounded, finely shaped) and 'major' (larger) . Origin: Oval area on the lower third of the posterior surface of the inferior angle of the scapula. Insertion: Medial lip of the intertubercular sulcus on the anterior surface of the humerus. Nerve: Lower subscapular nerve C5–7 . Action: Medial rotation and extension of the arm at the glenohumeral joint. Basic Functional Movement: Reaching into the back pocket . Referred Pain Patterns: Deep pain into the posterior glenohumeral joint and an oval zone (5–10 cm) of pain in the posterior deltoid area, which can radiate strongly to the long head of the biceps brachii. Diffuse pain also extends into the dorsum of the forearm .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Teres Major (Continued)
content: Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Indicated methods include spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, muscle energy, and positional release; wet needling is also mentioned . Advice: Use heat or warmth (hot showers), avoid heavy steering wheels, monitor gym activities, hug a pillow at night, and perform plenty of self-stretching . Indications: Frozen shoulder syndrome, pain reaching above the head, slight pain at rest, pain when driving, and impingement syndromes (sometimes misdiagnosed as thoracic outlet syndrome) . Self-Help Technique: Includes studying anatomy and using a pressure ball on the back of the head/trigger point for up to 10 minutes until pain eases .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Teres Major (Causes and Connections)
content: Causes: Sports-related forcefully overhead lifting, post-shoulder fracture/dislocation, frozen shoulder syndrome, sudden unexpected loading (e.g., a fall), and prolonged immobility in a sling . Differential Diagnosis: Impingement syndromes, rotator cuff tendinopathy, cervical neuropatterns (C6–C7), thoracic outlet syndrome, and supraspinatus calcification . Connections: Rhomboids, triceps brachii (long head), latissimus dorsi, teres minor, pectorals, posterior deltoid, C6 or C7 neck disc issues, and subdeltoid bursitis .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Biceps Brachii
content: Origin: Long head: supraglenoid tubercle of the scapula; Short head: tip of the coracoid process . Insertion: Radial tuberosity. Nerve: Musculocutaneous nerve C5, 6. Action: Powerful flexor of the forearm at the elbow and supinates the forearm (described as putting in a corkscrew and pulling out the cork). It is an accessory flexor of the arm at the glenohumeral joint . Basic Functional Movement: Picking up an object or bringing food to the mouth. Referred Pain Patterns: Localized pain with an intense ellipse superficially located over the long head tendon; also refers pain into the anterior cubital fossa .

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Shoulder Pain Treatment Sequence (NAT)
content: A detailed Step-by-Step NAT protocol for shoulder pain. STEP 1: Study anatomy and fiber direction of supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor/major, pectoralis minor, serratus anterior, subscapularis, coracobrachialis, deltoid, pectoralis major, and subclavius . STEP 2: Sitting ICT to supraspinatus and upper trapezius. STEP 3: Massage area generously. STEP 4: Side-lying ICT to deltoid (stroking upward only) and teres minor (letting hand fall off the bed). STEP 5: Supine ICT to pectoralis minor and infraspinatus (a Super Trigger Point). This step can be very painful, so deep breathing is advised .

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Diaphragm Self-Help and Release Techniques
content: Self-Help Technique involves reaching under the lower ribs and performing deep stroking massage from front to sides, which can be painful. Diaphragm and Mandibular Raphe Balanced Release Technique: 1. Position knees in flexion; 2. Locate trigger points along the bottom of the rib cage centrally; 3. Locate trigger points in the opposite ramus of the jaw; 4. Use deep breathing and steady pressure until relaxation is felt.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Low Back Pain Overview
content: Balanced Release Technique (Continued): Steps 5-6 involve moving laterally to the next point and repeating on the opposite side. The page lists posterior muscles including the Spinalis, Latissimus dorsi, Longissimus thoracis/lumborum, Iliocostalis thoracis/lumborum, and Multifidus. Indications: Low back pain is ubiquitous, affecting 70% of people and costing the US economy over $50bn annually in lost productivity and healthcare.

chapter: Muscles of the Trunk and Spine
title: Low Back Pain NAT Sequence and Shoulder Index
content: Step-by-step NAT protocol for Low Back Pain: STEP 2: Prone ICT to Gluteus medius (STP); STEP 3: Cross-fiber massage to low back; STEP 4: Prone ICT to Multifidus and lumbar erector spinae; STEP 5: Massage spinal muscles; STEP 6: Supine ICT to Rectus abdominis (outside edge) and anterior ramus of diaphragm; STEP 7: Repeat 3 times. Also includes an index for 'Regional Trigger Points for Shoulder and Upper Arm Pain'.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Rhomboids Overview
content: Treatment Protocol: Steps 4-6 involve applying sustained pressure until the trigger point softens, meeting the next barrier, and changing the direction of pressure for better results. Name Derivation: Greek 'rhomboeides' (parallelogram shaped). Description: Rhomboid major is often continuous with rhomboid minor; they are named for their shape.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Rhomboid Details
content: Origin: Minor: spinous processes of C7 and T1, lower ligamentum nuchae; Major: spinous processes of T2–5. Insertion: Minor: medial border of scapula at the spine; Major: medial border of scapula from the spine to the inferior angle. Nerve: Dorsal scapular nerve C4, 5. Action: Elevates and retracts the scapula.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Serratus Anterior
content: Origin: Lateral surfaces of upper eight or nine ribs. Insertion: Anterior surface of medial border of scapula. Nerve: Long thoracic nerve C5–7. Action: Rotates scapula for abduction/flexion; protracts scapula and holds it to the chest wall. Basic Functional Movement: Reaching forward for something barely in reach or pushing a door open.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Pectoralis Minor
content: Insertion: Coracoid process of scapula. Nerve: Medial pectoral nerve C5–T1. Action: Draws shoulder downward; protracts scapula; acts as an accessory muscle of inspiration. Basic Functional Movement: Pushing on the arms of a chair to stand up. Referred Pain Patterns: Chest flaring in muscle fiber direction; radiations into the medial upper extremity to the medial two and a half fingers.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Latissimus Dorsi
content: Treatment Note: Step 6 involves repeating the stroking in the opposite direction. Name Derivation: Latin 'latissimus' (widest) and 'dorsi' (of the back). Origin: Spinous processes of lower six thoracic vertebrae, lumbar vertebrae (via thoracolumbar fascia), iliac crest, and lower ribs. Insertion: Floor of intertubercular sulcus of humerus.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Supraspinatus
content: Treatment Note: Step 6 involves changing the direction of pressure. Name Derivation: Latin 'supra' (above) and 'spina' (spine). Description: A member of the rotator cuff. Origin: Medial two-thirds of supraspinous fossa of scapula. Insertion: Superior facet on the greater tubercle of humerus. Nerve: Suprascapular nerve C5, 6. Action: Initiates abduction of the arm to 15 degrees.

chapter: Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm
title: Teres Minor
content: Treatment Note: Step 6 involves changing the direction of pressure. Name Derivation: Latin 'teres' (rounded) and 'minor' (smaller). Description: A member of the rotator cuff. Origin: Upper two-thirds of the posterior surface of the scapula adjacent to the lateral border. Insertion: Inferior facet on greater tubercle of humerus. Nerve: Axillary nerve C5, 6. Action: Lateral rotation of the arm at the glenohumeral joint.

chapter: Muscles of the Forearm and Hand
title: Supinator
content: Treatment Note: Steps 6-8 involve isometric contraction held for 15–30 seconds, repeated 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'supinus' (lying on the back). Description: Supinator is almost entirely concealed by superficial muscles. Origin: Superficial part: lateral epicondyle of humerus, radial collateral and anular ligaments; Deep part: supinator crest of ulna. Insertion: Lateral surface of radius superior to the anterior oblique line. Nerve: Posterior interosseous nerve C5, 6, (7).

chapter: Muscles of the Forearm and Hand
title: Opponens Pollicis
content: Treatment Note: Steps 5-6 involve gently lengthening the muscle during relaxation and repeating usually 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'opponens' (opposing) and 'pollicis' (of the thumb). Description: Usually partly fused with flexor pollicis brevis and deep to abductor pollicis brevis. Origin: Flexor retinaculum and tubercle of trapezium. Insertion: Entire length of radial border of first metacarpal. Nerve: Recurrent branch of median nerve C8, T1.

chapter: Muscles of the Forearm and Hand
title: Wrist Pain Treatment Sequence (NAT)
content: A detailed Step-by-Step NAT protocol. STEP 1: Study anatomy and fiber direction. STEP 2: Sitting ICT to Scalenes (STP) and upper trapezius; use slow sliding massage downward onto rhomboids, pausing on trigger points. STEP 3: Massage area generously. STEP 4: Administer ICT to Infraspinatus, wrist flexors, wrist flexor common origin, wrist extensors, wrist extensor common origin, palmaris longus, interosseous membrane, and small muscles of the hand.

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Regional Trigger Points for Hip, Thigh, and Knee Pain
content: A regional index for muscles associated with hip, thigh, and knee pain, including: Adductors (192), Biceps femoris (190), Gastrocnemius (210), Gluteus maximus (178), Gluteus medius (182), Gluteus minimus (184), Iliopsoas (120), Obturator internus (188), Pectineus (194), Piriformis (186), Popliteus (216), Quadratus lumborum (118), Rectus abdominis (116), Rectus femoris (198), Sartorius (196), Hamstrings (190), Soleus (214), Tensor fasciae latae (180), and Vastus lateralis/medialis (198).

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Hip and Thigh Self-Help
content: Self-Help Technique: 1. Observe anatomy. 2. Run downward from the rim of the pelvis toward the front of the thigh, identifying painful spots. 3. Work using thumbs with small scooping movements. 4. Pause on knots until pain subsides; pressure may be increased. 5. Follow the stroke to the end. Indications: Pain in low back/buttocks, night pain, pain on side lying, post-hip/spinal surgery, sitting on a wallet, leg length discrepancy, arthritic hip, and pregnancy.

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Piriformis
content: Treatment Note: Steps 6-8 involve isometric contraction held for 15–30 seconds, repeated 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'pirum' (pear) and 'forma' (shape). Description: Leaves the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen; along with obturator internus, it is a muscle of the pelvic wall. Origin: Anterior surface of sacrum between anterior sacral foramina. Insertion: Medial side of superior border of greater trochanter. Nerve: Branches from sacral nerves S1, 2. Action: Laterally rotates extended femur; abducts flexed femur; holds femur head in acetabulum.

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Obturator Internus and Short Lateral Rotators
content: Treatment Note: Steps 4-6 involve relaxing the muscle, gently lengthening it, and repeating usually 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'obturare' (to obstruct) and 'internus' (internal). Description: The group includes Gemellus superior/inferior, obturator internus/externus, and quadratus femoris. These muscles work together kinetically, starting at the top of the femur and inserting on the posterior femur and greater trochanter. Pain is felt locally at the greater trochanter, over the buttock into the groin, and in the posterior thigh.

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Pectineus
content: Treatment Note: Step 6 involves repeating the stroking in the opposite direction. Name Derivation: Latin 'pecten' (comb) and 'pectinatus' (comb shaped). Description: Sandwiched between psoas major and adductor longus. Origin: Pecten pubis and adjacent pelvic bone. Insertion: Oblique line from base of lesser trochanter to linea aspera of femur. Nerve: Femoral nerve L2, 3. Action: Adducts and flexes thigh. Basic Functional Movement: Walking in a straight line. Referred Pain Patterns: Strong 8–12 cm zone in anterior groin with diffuse radiations toward the anteromedial thigh.

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Sartorius
content: Name Derivation: Latin 'sartor' (tailor). Description: The most superficial muscle of the anterior thigh and the longest strap muscle in the body. Its medial border forms the lateral boundary of the femoral triangle. Action: Puts the lower limbs in the seated cross-legged position of a tailor. Origin: Anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Quadriceps (Rectus Femoris and Vastus Lateralis)
content: Treatment Note: Steps 5-6 involve increasing pressure on trigger points and changing the direction of pressure. Name Derivation: Latin 'rectus' (straight), 'femoris' (of the thigh), 'vastus' (vast), and 'lateralis' (relating to the side). Description: The four quadriceps are rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius. All cross the knee joint. Rectus femoris has two origins (reflected and straight heads), the latter unique to humans due to upright posture.

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Hamstrings Overview
content: The differential diagnosis for hamstrings includes sciatica, radiculopathy, muscle tears, osteitis, bursitic osteoarthritis of the knee, knee joint dysfunction, and tenosynovitis . It is connected to the piriformis, popliteus, gluteals, obturator internus, vastus lateralis, plantaris, gastrocnemius, and thoracolumbar paraspinal muscles . The page outlines the Contract-Relax-Antagonist-Contract (CRAC) technique as a combined neuromodulatory input of post-isometric relaxation and reciprocal inhibition, excellent for chronic conditions [1, 2].

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Adductors
content: Includes the adductor brevis, longus, and magnus . Basic Functional Movement: Bringing the second leg in or out of a car . Referred Pain Patterns: (1) two zones localized around the anterior hip and above the knee (5–8 cm); (2) the whole anteromedial thigh from the inguinal ligament to the medial knee joint; (3) the medial thigh from the hip to the knee . High adductor magnus trigger points can also refer pain to the pelvic region .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Adductors Overview
content: The (Inhibition) Compression Technique is detailed for active central trigger points [5, 6]. The practitioner applies gradually increasing pressure to the trigger point while lengthening the host muscle until a palpable barrier is hit, experienced as discomfort but not pain . Pressure is sustained until the point softens and is then repeated to reach the next barrier .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Pectineus
content: Name Derivation: Latin 'pecten' (comb) and 'pectinatus' (comb shaped) . Description: Sandwiched between the psoas major and adductor longus . Origin: Pecten pubis and adjacent bone of pelvis . Insertion: Oblique line from the base of the lesser trochanter to the linea aspera of the femur . Nerve: Femoral nerve L2, 3 . Action: Adducts and flexes the thigh at the hip joint . Basic Functional Movement: Walking along a straight line . Referred Pain Patterns: Strong 8–12 cm zone of pain in the anterior groin with diffuse radiations in an oval toward the anteromedial thigh .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Pectineus Overview
content: Treatment includes the Deep Stroking Massage Technique. Step 5: Apply sustained pressure until the trigger point softens, continuing the stroke toward the attachment of the taut band . Step 6: Repeat the stroking in the opposite direction . Illustrations also show the supine inhibition of the pectineus using the elbow for a stronger lever .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Sartorius
content: Description: The most superficial muscle of the anterior thigh and the longest strap muscle in the body; its medial border forms the lateral boundary of the femoral triangle . Action: Puts the lower limbs in the seated cross-legged position . Origin: Anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) . Connections include the vastus medialis, biceps femoris, gracilis, pectineus, and tensor fasciae latae .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Sartorius Overview
content: Utilizes the (Inhibition) Compression Technique. Step 4: Apply sustained pressure until the trigger point softens, which can take from a few seconds to several minutes . Self-help advice includes reviewing anatomy, identifying the trigger point, pausing on it until it softens, and massaging the area afterwards [10, 11].

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Quadriceps
content: Description: Comprises the rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius; all four cross the knee joint . Rectus femoris has two origins (reflected and straight heads), the latter being unique to humans due to our upright posture . Trigger points here are known for causing a 'buckling knee' sensation .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Quadriceps Overview
content: Treatment notes emphasize increasing pressure on trigger points and changing the direction of pressure for better outcomes . Methods indicated include spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, and positional release . Self-stretching is advised thereafter three times per day .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Pelvic Pain
content: Indications: Pain during sexual intercourse, cramping or sharp pains, heaviness or a feeling of pressure inside the pelvis, extreme or constant pain, dull ache, pain during bowel movements, and dysmenorrhea . NAT Step-by-Step Protocol: STEP 1: Study anatomy; STEP 2: Prone ICT to inferior Gluteus maximus, Piriformis, Biceps femoris origin, spinal muscles, and pelvic floor muscles; STEP 3: Side-lying ICT to Pectineus and Adductor magnus origin; STEP 4: Supine ICT to lower Rectus abdominis (STP) and Psoas major .

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Regional Trigger Points for Hip, Thigh, and Knee Pain
content: This regional index lists muscles associated with pain in the hip, thigh, and knee, along with their page references: Adductor brevis (192), Adductor longus (192), Adductor magnus (192), Biceps femoris (190), Fibularis longus (208), Flexor digitorum brevis (222), Gastrocnemius (210), Gluteus maximus (178), Gluteus medius (182), Gluteus minimus (184), Iliocostalis lumborum (102), Iliocostalis thoracis (102), Iliopsoas (120), Obturator internus (188), Pectineus (194), Piriformis (186), Popliteus (216), Quadratus lumborum (118), Rectus abdominis (116), Rectus femoris (198), Sartorius (196), Semimembranosus (190), Semitendinosus (190), Soleus (214), Tensor fasciae latae (180), Vastus lateralis (198), and Vastus medialis (198).

chapter: Muscles of the Hip and Thigh
title: Knee Pain Treatment Sequence (NAT)
content: The step-by-step NAT protocol for knee pain includes: STEP 1: Study the anatomy and fiber direction of the quadriceps (vastus lateralis, rectus femoris, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis) and popliteus. STEP 2: Prone ICT (Inhibition Compression Technique) to the popliteus. STEP 3: Deep stroking massage to the area generously, moving upward only. STEP 4: Perform supine medial and lateral gapping articulation to the knee joint with slight flexion; followed by ICT to the vastus medialis/lateralis insertions, the quadriceps expansion (above the patella), and the ligamentum patellae (a Super Trigger Point) just below the patella.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Regional Trigger Points for Leg, Ankle, and Foot Pain
content: This index provides references for muscles related to lower leg and foot pain: Abductor digiti minimi (222), Abductor hallucis (222), Adductor brevis (192), Adductor hallucis (224), Adductor longus (192), Extensor digitorum brevis (222), Extensor digitorum longus (206), Extensor hallucis longus (206), Fibulares (208), Flexor digitorum brevis (222), Flexor digitorum longus (218), Flexor hallucis brevis (224), Gastrocnemius (210), Gluteus minimus (182), Interossei (224), Plantaris (212), Popliteus (216), Quadratus plantae (224), Semimembranosus (190), Semitendinosus (190), Soleus (214), Tibialis anterior (204), Tibialis posterior (220), and Vastus lateralis (198).

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Tibialis Anterior
content: Origin: Lateral surface of the tibia and the adjacent interosseous membrane. Insertion: Medial and inferior surfaces of the medial cuneiform and adjacent surfaces on the base of the first metatarsal. Nerve: Deep fibular nerve L4, 5. Action: Dorsiflexes the foot at the ankle joint and inverts the foot; providing dynamic support to the medial arch. Basic Functional Movement: Facilitates walking and running by preventing the foot from slapping the ground after heel strike and lifting the foot clear during the swing phase.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Tibialis Anterior Treatment Note
content: Step 6 of the treatment procedure: To achieve a better result, practitioners are encouraged to change the direction of pressure during repetitions.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Extensor Digitorum Longus and Extensor Hallucis Longus
content: Description: Like the hand, the EDL forms extensor hoods on the dorsum of the proximal phalanges, joined by lumbrical tendons and the extensor digitorum brevis. Origin (EDL): Proximal half of the medial surface of the fibula and lateral tibial condyle. Origin (EHL): Middle half of the medial surface of the fibula and the interosseous membrane. Insertion (EDL): Along the dorsal surface of the four lateral toes, attaching to the bases of middle and distal phalanges. Insertion (EHL): Base of the distal phalanx of the great toe. Nerve: Deep fibular nerve L5, S1. Action (EDL): Extends the lateral four toes and dorsiflexes the foot. Action (EHL): Extends the great toe and dorsiflexes the foot.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Extensor Digitorum/Hallucis Treatment (PIR)
content: The Post-Isometric (PIR) Technique for subacute to chronic settings: 1. Identify the trigger point. 2. Position the patient comfortably for a full stretch of the host muscle. 3. Using 10–25% power, have the patient contract the muscle at its maximal pain-free length against isometric resistance for 3–10 seconds. 4. Ask the patient to relax. 5. During relaxation, gently lengthen the muscle passive to the point of resistance. 6. Repeat several times (usually three).

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Fibulares
content: Name Derivation: Latin fibula (pin/buckle); comprising fibularis longus, brevis, and tertius. Description: The insertion of the fibularis longus helps maintain the transverse and lateral longitudinal arches of the foot. A slip from the fibularis brevis often joins the EDL tendon for the little toe, sometimes called the peroneus digiti minimi.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Fibulares Overview and Treatment
content: Connections: Tensor fasciae latae (TFL), gluteus minimus, extensor digitorum longus/brevis, and extensor hallucis brevis. Treatment Protocol: Utilizes the Post-Isometric (PIR) Technique (Steps 1–6) as described for the extensors, focusing on subacute to chronic settings. Step 6 involves repeating the lengthening phase three times.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Gastrocnemius
content: Name Derivation: Greek gaster (stomach) and kneme (lower leg). Origin: Medial head arises from the posterior surface of the distal femur just superior to the medial condyle; Lateral head arises from the upper postero-lateral surface of the lateral femoral condyle. Insertion: Posterior surface of the calcaneus via the Achilles (calcaneal) tendon.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Ankle Pain Treatment Sequence (NAT)
content: Step-by-step NAT protocol for ankle pain: STEP 2: Prone articulation into inversion and eversion of the talocrural joint. STEP 3: Prone ICT to the gastrocnemius, soleus, plantaris, and tibialis posterior. STEP 4: Side-lying deep stroking massage to the fibulares; thorough supine ICT to the extensor digitorum brevis, extensor digitorum longus tendon (a Super Trigger Point), and the anterior talocrural joint.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Foot Pain Treatment Sequence and Referred Pain Patterns
content: Indications: Heel pain, metatarsalgia, plantar fasciitis, myalgia, sesamoiditis, heel spurs, and soreness on walking or at rest. STEP 1: Study anatomy and fiber direction. STEP 2: Side-lying ICT to the medial head of the gastrocnemius and the lower points of the soleus. STEP 3: Massage the area generously. The page also features comprehensive diagrams of referred pain patterns for the lower leg and foot, including the gastrocnemius heads, soleus, plantaris, popliteus, and toe flexors/extensors.

chapter: Muscles of the Leg and Foot
title: Foot Pain Sequence (Continued)
content: STEP 4: Prone ICT to the tibialis posterior and flexor digitorum longus (a Super Trigger Point). STEP 5: Thorough supine ICT to the plantar fascia (starting at the heel) and small foot muscles; specifically target the gristly lumpy nodules often found around heel spurs. The page concludes with the beginning of the 'Glossary of Anatomical Directions', starting with Abduction: movement away from the midline.
